core.intro: Starfruit, or carambola, is a fast-growing tropical tree that rewards the right spot: warm and frost-free, full sun, free-draining soil kept steadily moist, a named grafted variety, and a plan for fruit fly. Get those right and a single tree can carry several crops a year. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply wherever you can grow the tree.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Carambola splits into two broad camps: sweet, low-acid types for fresh eating, and tart types better suited to juice and cooking. For a home tree, pick a sweet, named, grafted cultivar. The ones sold in Australia include Sweet Gold (a sweet, low-acid selection bred for fresh eating), Kembangan (the large, sweet Malaysian Sri Kembangan), Thai Knight (sweet and productive, a favourite in commercial Queensland plantings), Arkin (the Florida commercial variety, attractive gold fruit that holds and packs well), Kary (a very good flavoured Hawaiian type) and Fwang Tung (a Thai variety, very sweet and crisp like an apple even when slightly green, though its large soft wings bruise easily). As a rule the pale, greenish or white-fleshed types are lower in acid and can be eaten before they are fully ripe, while the deep yellow and orange types are best left to colour fully. The in-stock table above shows which named varieties are available right now and what they cost.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Carambola flowers are heterostylous, which means a given variety carries flowers with either a short style or a long style. Short-style cultivars (Kembangan, Fwang Tung and Wheeler among them) are often partly self-incompatible and set their best, best-shaped crops when a long-style cultivar (Arkin, Kary or Maha) grows nearby, while long-style types are reliably self-fertile. In practice a single tree of a good variety usually still crops well, because only about half a percent of the flowers on a tree need to set to give a heavy crop, and bees and other insects do the pollinating. So one tree is fine for most gardens, but if you have room for two different cultivars you will lift fruit set and get fewer distorted fruit (Fwang Tung in particular can throw misshapen fruit when poorly pollinated). For a single tree, lean on a self-fruitful variety such as Arkin or Kary.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Plant in full sun in the warmest, most frost-free, wind-sheltered spot you have. Carambola grows fast and its wood is brittle, so limbs snap and fruit drops in strong wind; a windbreak or a sheltered corner pays off, especially in the first few years. It is not fussy about soil and will grow on anything from sand to clay loam (deep, well-drained clay loams are ideal), and it even shrugs off short spells of waterlogging better than most tropical fruit, but it will not thrive where water ponds for long. Slightly acid soil suits it best, around pH 4.5 to 6.5; above pH 7 the tree struggles to take up iron, magnesium and manganese and the leaves yellow, which is the thing to watch on alkaline or limey soils. Dig compost or aged manure into the planting site, mulch well, and keep the young tree watered (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Carambola has a high water demand and only limited tolerance of drought, so the soil should rarely dry out. Keep young trees damp to get them established; once a tree is bearing, the make-or-break window is flowering through fruit fill, when a dry spell will drop flowers and small fruit. In dry weather the Queensland guidance is to irrigate at roughly nine-tenths of pan evaporation, about 30 to 50 mm of water a week, and to mulch to hold moisture. There is one useful quirk: a short, deliberate dry spell can trigger a flowering flush, which growers use to time crops, but do not let a cropping tree go hard dry. For feeding, use a balanced fertiliser little and often through the warm season. The Florida home-garden program is a mix around 6 to 8 percent nitrogen, 2 to 4 percent phosphorus and 6 to 8 percent potassium with 3 to 4 percent magnesium, at roughly 115 to 225 g a feed for a young tree every one to two months, building to between half a kilogram and a couple of kilograms on a bearing tree spread over four to six feeds a year; the older Queensland program splits nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium across three dressings scaled to tree age, plus dolomite. Magnesium and the trace elements are the ones to keep an eye on, especially zinc, and iron and manganese on alkaline soil, where a foliar feed is the quickest fix for yellowing leaves.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: A grafted tree usually starts bearing within a year or two of planting, and in warm areas can carry more than one crop a year. Fruit takes roughly 60 to 75 days from fruit set to maturity. The single most important thing to know is that carambola does not get any sweeter after picking: it does not ripen up on the bench the way a mango or banana does, so it has to be picked ripe. Harvest when the green skin turns to the variety's full yellow or gold colour in the grooves between the ribs, while the very tips of the ribs may still show a touch of green; sweet, low-acid types like Fwang Tung can be taken slightly green, while the deep yellow types are best fully coloured. Pick by hand and handle the fruit gently, because the thin wing edges bruise and brown quickly. Stored in a bag in the fridge the fruit keeps for around two to three weeks. Thinning the young fruit while it is 20 to 50 mm long improves the size and sweetness of what is left and reduces the limb breakage that comes from a tree overbearing.

sections.heading: Eating and food safety
sections.body: You eat carambola fresh, the whole fruit including its thin waxy skin, most famously sliced crossways into stars for fruit platters, drinks and salads, or pressed for juice. How sweet it tastes depends on the variety and on ripeness, because the sugar-to-acid balance shifts as the fruit colours up: sour and unripe fruit is far higher in oxalic acid than a sweet, ripe one, which is another reason to pick a sweet cultivar and let it colour fully. One genuine caution applies to eating it, whatever the variety: star fruit contains oxalates and a natural neurotoxin called caramboxin, and people with kidney disease can be seriously harmed by even a small amount, so anyone with reduced kidney function is advised to avoid star fruit entirely. For healthy people eating it in normal amounts it is a safe, refreshing fruit.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling. Carambola does not come true or predictably from seed, and seedlings are slow to bear and variable in quality, while a grafted named tree fruits within a year or two and to type. It is hard to strike from cuttings or air layers, so grafting onto a seedling rootstock is the standard method, and a grafted tree is what reputable nurseries sell. Check the label for the cultivar name, and pick a sweet one if you want it for fresh eating. A cheap unnamed seedling is fine if you only want the tree itself, but do not count on the fruit. The in-stock table above lists the named varieties available now and their prices across the nurseries we track.

faqs.q: How big does a starfruit tree get, and can I keep it small or grow it in a pot?
faqs.a: Left alone it makes a small to medium tree, about 7 to 10 metres tall and nearly as wide, but it takes pruning well and is easily held to 2 to 3 metres for a backyard or for netting and picking. A young tree grows happily in a large pot for several years, which suits a courtyard or a cooler climate where you need to move it under cover in winter; use a free-draining mix, water often because pots dry out fast, and feed regularly.

faqs.q: Is star fruit the same as carambola, and what is bilimbi?
faqs.a: Yes, star fruit and carambola are two names for the same fruit, Averrhoa carambola, and you will see both on nursery labels. Bilimbi (Averrhoa bilimbi) is its close cousin: a small, very sour green fruit used for cooking, pickles and drinks rather than eaten fresh off the tree. So do not confuse the sweet star fruit you probably want with the intensely acidic bilimbi, which is a different tree.

faqs.q: Why is my starfruit tree dropping its small fruit?
faqs.a: A degree of natural shedding after a heavy set is normal, and a young tree sheds more than a settled one. The most common cause you can fix is letting the tree go dry while it is flowering and sizing fruit, so steady moisture and mulch through that period make the biggest difference. On the short-style varieties that need a pollinator, a second cultivar nearby steadies the set, and a tree carrying too much will shed and risk snapping limbs, which is why thinning the young crop helps.

faqs.q: What does star fruit taste like, and which variety is sweetest?
faqs.a: Ripe star fruit is crisp, very juicy and refreshing, somewhere between tart and sweet rather than intensely sugary (its sugar level is modest, well below a mango's), with an edible waxy skin so you eat it whole, sliced into stars for salads, garnishes and juice. For the sweetest fresh eating choose a low-acid cultivar such as Sweet Gold or Fwang Tung; the tarter types are better juiced or cooked.

QLD.intro: Growing starfruit in Queensland. Queensland is the home of Australian carambola: it has been grown in the tropical north for well over a century, and the warm, frost-free coast is where the crop performs best.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Carambola wants warm, frost-free conditions and does best on the coast below about 300 metres. Its preferred cropping country is the tropical north, where trees fruit through much of the year, but it crops well right down the warm coast and into the subtropical south-east, just with one or two main flushes a year rather than continuous fruiting. Frost is the hard limit and young trees are tender, so the cool, frosty inland and the granite highlands are not carambola country.

sections.heading: Where starfruit grows in Queensland
sections.body: The heart of production is the Wet Tropics around Innisfail, Tully, Babinda and the Cassowary Coast, the Atherton Tableland and Mareeba, and the Cairns district, with more along the warm central coast around Townsville and Bowen. From there it thins south as a backyard and market-garden tree through the subtropical south-east. As a rough rule it is most at home north of Rockhampton.

sections.heading: Harvest window in Queensland
sections.body: In the tropical north there are two main production peaks, broadly December to February and June to August, with lighter pickings in between and a quieter gap around September to November. In the cooler subtropical south the main flowering is over summer and the fruit then takes 10 to 16 weeks to mature, so the bulk of the crop comes through late summer and autumn. Individual trees can flower over a long spell, so harvest is often drawn out.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Queensland
sections.body: Fruit fly is the number one problem. Queensland fruit fly is endemic and uses carambola as a host, so plan to bag the fruit, run baits or net the tree. Fruit-piercing moth and leaf-roller caterpillars also go for the fruit and foliage, and in the humid wet-season north the leaf-spot fungi (Cercospora) and the red alga Cephaleuros can mark leaves and fruit. Flying foxes, which trouble so many tropical fruits, are not usually a carambola problem.

faqs.q: Can I grow starfruit in Brisbane or southern Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. The subtropical south-east is cooler than the tropical north, so expect one or two flushes and a late-summer-to-autumn crop rather than year-round fruit, but a tree in a warm, frost-free, sheltered spot crops well. Choose a sweet, self-fruitful variety so a single tree sets, keep the water up, and have a fruit fly plan.

faqs.q: Will birds or flying foxes take my starfruit in Queensland?
faqs.a: Flying foxes are not the headache for carambola that they are for many tropical fruits, but cockatoos and other birds will help themselves, and fruit-piercing moth and fruit fly do real damage. Bagging the fruit or netting the tree solves the lot at once, and bagging doubles as fruit fly protection.

NSW.intro: Growing starfruit in New South Wales. NSW is the southern edge of outdoor carambola: it crops in the frost-free, subtropical far north of the coast, and becomes a sheltered-courtyard or greenhouse plant the further south you go.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Carambola needs a warm, frost-free position, and that limits dependable outdoor cropping to the subtropical far north coast. Even there the cooler winters slow the tree and squeeze fruiting into one main season instead of the near-continuous cropping of the tropics. South of the mid-north coast, cold nights and frost turn it into a pot or glasshouse subject rather than a garden tree.

sections.heading: Where starfruit grows in NSW
sections.body: The practical range is the subtropical Northern Rivers: Tweed, Murwillumbah, Lismore and Ballina, and on down the Coffs Harbour coast. Warm coastal pockets further south, including sheltered parts of the Sydney basin, can carry a tree in a frost-free, sun-trapped corner, but crops there are light and late. Cold or inland districts are not suited.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: With effectively a single main flowering carried over summer, the fruit matures mostly through late summer and autumn, broadly February to April, later and lighter than in tropical Queensland. A cool early winter can leave the last fruit small and less sweet, so the earlier-set crop is the pick of the season.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established right along the NSW coast and will sting carambola, so bagging or netting is wise wherever you are. Fruit-piercing moth and leaf-roller caterpillars turn up too, and damp coastal springs and autumns bring leaf-spot onto the foliage. Dry inland air would cut the fungal pressure, but inland is too cold and frosty for the tree in the first place.

faqs.q: Will starfruit survive frost on the NSW coast?
faqs.a: Only just, and only when young trees are protected. Light frost near zero degrees burns the young leaves, and a harder frost can kill a young tree outright, so it needs a genuinely frost-free, north-facing, wind-sheltered spot. In the Northern Rivers that is easy to find; around Sydney and further south you are relying on a warm coastal microclimate or growing it in a pot you can move under cover for winter.

faqs.q: Which starfruit variety suits the NSW coast?
faqs.a: In a shorter, cooler season a self-fruitful variety that does not depend on a pollinator partner is the safe choice, so a single tree still sets a crop. Arkin and Sweet Gold are good picks: both are reliably self-fertile and sweet, which matters more here than chasing the heaviest-cropping commercial types built for the tropics.

WA.intro: Growing starfruit in Western Australia. Carambola is a tropical tree in a state with very little tropical coast, so in WA it is a crop for the warm, frost-free north, plus a handful of sheltered Perth microclimates, and live trees have to clear WA quarantine to get here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: WA's reliable carambola country is the tropical and subtropical north. The Ord around Kununurra and the wider Kimberley, the Pilbara coast, and the warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon can all fruit it for much of the year, just as the Top End does across the border. The tree is cold-hardy only to about 6 degrees and is frost-tender, so in Mediterranean Perth it is marginal: it will grow and can crop in a warm, frost-free, sheltered coastal or northern-suburbs spot, but it dislikes Perth's dry summer air and salt-laden winds and needs steady watering to do anything at all.

sections.heading: Where starfruit grows in WA
sections.body: Think north first: the Ord River irrigation area at Kununurra and the Kimberley, the Carnarvon and Gascoyne coast, and the Pilbara, where the warmth suits it. In the south it is a backyard novelty for the warmest, most frost-free pockets of the Perth coast and inner northern suburbs, grown in a sheltered sun-trap or a large pot rather than out in the open.

sections.heading: Soil and water in WA
sections.body: Perth's alkaline, lime-rich coastal sands are the particular challenge here. Above about pH 7 carambola cannot take up enough iron, magnesium and manganese and its leaves yellow, so on Perth sand you have to work at it: dig in plenty of compost, mulch heavily to hold moisture and feed the soil, keep the water up through the dry summer, and correct yellowing fast with a foliar iron and zinc feed. In the north, deep tropical soils are easier, but the high water demand still has to be met by irrigation through the dry.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's dry heat keeps the leaf-spot fungi that plague the humid east largely in check, which is one advantage of growing it here. The standout pest is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established across the south-west and the Gascoyne and will use carambola as a host, so bait, bag or net the fruit. In the tropical north you face the same fruit fly and fruit-piercing moth pressure as the neighbouring Northern Territory.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from the eastern states must meet import conditions under the WA Organism List and are often refused entry or treated on arrival, so many interstate nurseries simply will not post trees here. The in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can ship to WA, which in practice means the larger mail-order nurseries that hold a WA-entry protocol.

faqs.q: Can you grow starfruit in Perth?
faqs.a: You can, but treat it as marginal. Give it the warmest, most frost-free, wind-sheltered coastal or northern-suburbs spot, improve and mulch the alkaline sand, keep the water steady through summer, and feed trace elements to stop the leaves yellowing. Expect lighter, more seasonal crops than the tropical north, and consider a large pot you can shelter through a cold snap. The Kimberley, Pilbara and Gascoyne are far better suited than Perth.

faqs.q: Can I bring a starfruit tree into WA from the eastern states myself?
faqs.a: Not freely. Live plants entering WA have to meet the state's quarantine import conditions, so you cannot simply carry or post a tree across the border. The simplest path is to buy from a nursery that is permitted to ship live plants to WA (the in-stock table is filtered to those), or to source a WA-grown tree locally.

VIC.intro: Growing starfruit in Victoria. Victoria is too cold and frosty for carambola to crop outdoors, so here it is a greenhouse, conservatory or large-pot plant, grown as much for novelty and foliage as for fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Carambola is a tropical tree that essentially stops growing below about 18 degrees and is set back by even light frost, with young leaves burnt near zero degrees and whole young trees killed by a hard frost. Victoria's cold winters rule out dependable outdoor fruiting anywhere in the state. In the very warmest, frost-free coastal pockets a tree may survive outside, but it will sulk through the cold months and rarely ripen a worthwhile crop.

sections.heading: Growing starfruit under cover in Victoria
sections.body: The realistic way to grow carambola in Victoria is under cover: a heated glasshouse or a bright conservatory, or a large pot that spends summer outdoors in full sun and moves into a warm, frost-free sunroom or glasshouse for winter. Keep it warm, humid, brightly lit and never let it chill or dry out. Even then, treat it as a novelty and foliage plant that may give you light, occasional fruit rather than a reliable harvest.

sections.heading: Pests under cover in Victoria
sections.body: Indoors the pests are the usual glasshouse ones rather than fruit fly: scale, mealybug and red-banded thrips on the foliage, and spider mite in dry, heated air. Keeping the humidity up discourages mite and keeps the tree happier. The upside of growing under cover is that the fruit fly and fruit-piercing moth that dominate in the warm states are not part of the picture.

faqs.q: Can starfruit survive a Melbourne winter outside?
faqs.a: No, not reliably. Frost and the long cold spell below the temperatures carambola needs will stop it cropping and can kill a young tree. To grow it in Victoria, keep it in a large pot that lives outside for the warm months and comes into a bright, frost-free sunroom or glasshouse for winter, or grow it permanently in a heated glasshouse.

faqs.q: Is a starfruit tree worth having in Melbourne?
faqs.a: As a greenhouse hobby or a novelty pot plant for the foliage and the odd fruit, it can be fun. For a dependable harvest, no, the climate is against it. If you want a subtropical fruit that actually crops outdoors this far south, a tamarillo or a feijoa is a far better bet.
